meta.title: Pride and Prejudice — Chapters 1-3 (Jane Austen, 1813; public domain, Project Gutenberg #1342)

characters.name: Mr. Bennet
characters.arc: Engages in banter with his wife about Mr. Bingley's arrival. / Reveals he has already visited Mr. Bingley, surprising his family. / 对舞会结果好奇，但希望妻子失望

characters.name: Mrs. Bennet
characters.arc: Eager to have Mr. Bingley marry one of her daughters. / From resentment to joy upon learning of Mr. Bennet's visit. / 对Mr. Bingley充满希望，对Mr. Darcy极为反感

characters.name: Mr. Bingley
characters.arc: Introduced as a wealthy single man who has taken Netherfield Park. / Subject of discussion; not present in chapter. / 成为舞会焦点，对Jane Bennet特别关注

characters.name: Lizzy
characters.arc: Mentions meeting Mr. Bingley at the assemblies. / 因Mr. Darcy的轻视而对他产生反感

characters.name: Jane
characters.arc: 受到Mr. Bingley青睐，感到满足

characters.name: Lydia
characters.arc: Expresses confidence about dancing with Mr. Bingley.

characters.name: Kitty
characters.arc: Scolded for coughing; asks about the next ball.

characters.name: Mary
traits: 被提及为最博学的女孩
characters.arc: Wishes to say something sensible but cannot.

characters.name: Mr. Darcy
characters.arc: 因傲慢态度引起众人反感，尤其得罪Elizabeth Bennet

timeline.label: by the end of next week
evening after the visit was paid

scenes.synopsis: Mr. Bennet and Mrs. Bennet discuss the arrival of a wealthy single man in the neighbourhood.

elements.text: A book lies open on Mr. Bennet's lap. He glances up from the page, a dry smile playing on his lips.

elements.line: My dear Mr. Bennet, have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last?

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet sets down her sewing, her eyes bright with excitement.

elements.line: Indeed I have. Mrs. Long says that Netherfield is taken by a young man of large fortune from the north of England.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet returns to his book, feigning indifference.

elements.line: Is that his name?
Bingley.
Is he married or single?
Oh! single, my dear, to be sure! A single man of large fortune; four or five thousand a year. What a fine thing for our girls!

elements.text: Mr. Bennet raises an eyebrow, a hint of amusement in his eyes.

elements.line: How so? How can it affect them?
My dear Mr. Bennet, how can you be so tiresome! You must know that I am thinking of his marrying one of them.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet closes his book slowly, a knowing look on his face.

elements.line: Is that his design in settling here?

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet huffs, exasperated.

elements.line: Design! Nonsense, how can you talk so! But it is very likely that he may fall in love with one of them, and therefore you must visit him as soon as he comes.

I see no occasion for that. You and the girls may go, or you may send them by themselves, which perhaps will be still better, for as you are as handsome as any of them, Mr. Bingley might like you the best of the party.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet flushes with annoyance, but a flicker of vanity crosses her face.

elements.line: My dear, you flatter me. I certainly have had my share of beauty, but I do not pretend to be anything extraordinary now. When a woman has five grown-up daughters, she ought to give over thinking of her own beauty.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet chuckles softly, returning to his book.

elements.line: In such cases, a woman has not often much beauty to think of.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet's mouth tightens. She picks up her sewing again, determined.

elements.line: But, my dear, you must indeed go and see Mr. Bingley when he comes into the neighbourhood.
It is more than I engage for, I assure you.

But consider your daughters. Only think what an establishment it would be for one of them. Sir William and Lady Lucas are determined to go, merely on that account, for in general you know they visit no newcomers. Indeed you must go, for it will be impossible for us to visit him if you do not.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet sighs, a glint of mischief in his eye.

elements.line: You are over-scrupulous, surely. I dare say Mr. Bingley will be very glad to see you; and I will send a few lines by you to assure him of my hearty consent to his marrying whichever he chooses of the girls; though I must throw in a good word for my little Lizzy.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet's eyes narrow, but she says nothing, turning away.
FADE OUT.

scenes.synopsis: Mrs. Bennet tells Mr. Bennet that Netherfield Park has been let to a wealthy young man.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet enters the library where Mr. Bennet is reading. She stands before him, hands clasped.

elements.line: My dear Mr. Bennet, have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last?

elements.text: Mr. Bennet looks up from his book, then returns to reading.

elements.line: I have not.
But it is; for Mrs. Long has just been here, and she told me all about it.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet turns a page, silent.

elements.line: Do not you want to know who has taken it?
You want to tell me, and I have no objection to hearing it.

scenes.synopsis: Mrs. Bennet excitedly tells Mr. Bennet about Mr. Bingley's arrival and fortune.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet, seated in his armchair, looks up from his book with a faint, knowing smile.

elements.line: Why, my dear, you must know, Mrs. Long says that Netherfield is taken by a young man of large fortune from the north of England; that he came down on Monday in a chaise and four to see the place, and was so much delighted with it that he agreed with Mr. Morris immediately; that he is to take possession before Michaelmas, and some of his servants are to be in the house by the end of next week.

scenes.synopsis: Mr. Bennet learns Bingley's name and marital status; Mrs. Bennet urges him to visit.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet closes his book, one finger marking his place. He looks at his wife with a faint, teasing smile.

elements.line: What is his name?
Bingley.
Is he married or single?
Oh, single, my dear, to be sure! A single man of large fortune; four or five thousand a year. What a fine thing for our girls!

elements.text: Mr. Bennet raises an eyebrow, his tone dry.

elements.line: How so? How can it affect them?
My dear Mr. Bennet, how can you be so tiresome? You must know that I am thinking of his marrying one of them.
Is that his design in settling here?
Design? Nonsense, how can you talk so! But it is very likely that he may fall in love with one of them, and therefore you must visit him as soon as he comes.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet opens his book again, deliberately avoiding her gaze.

elements.line: I see no occasion for that. You and the girls may go—or you may send them by themselves, which perhaps will be still better, for as you are as handsome as any of them, Mr. Bingley might like you the best of the party.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet's face flushes with irritation. She opens her mouth to retort, but Mr. Bennet has already returned to his reading, a slight smirk on his lips.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mr. Bennet teases Mrs. Bennet about her beauty, then she insists he must visit Bingley.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet looks up from his book, a teasing glint in his eye.

elements.line: As you are as handsome as any of them, Mr. Bingley might like you the best of the party.

My dear, you flatter me. I certainly have had my share of beauty, but I do not pretend to be anything extraordinary now. When a woman has five grown-up daughters, she ought to give over thinking of her own beauty.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet smirks, returning his gaze to his book.

elements.line: In such cases, a woman has not often much beauty to think of.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet's smile fades. She purses her lips, then leans forward earnestly.

elements.line: But I must insist on your visiting Mr. Bingley. It is the least you can do for our girls.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet sighs, closing his book with a snap.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mrs. Bennet insists Mr. Bennet visit Mr. Bingley; he jokes about consenting to Bingley marrying any daughter, favoring Lizzy.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet bustles into the library, where Mr. Bennet sits reading. She wrings her hands.

elements.line: But, my dear, you must indeed go and see Mr. Bingley when he comes into the neighbourhood.
It is more than I engage for, I assure you.

But consider your daughters. Only think what an establishment it would be for one of them. Sir William and Lady Lucas are determined to go, merely on that account; for in general, you know, they visit no new comers. Indeed you must go, for it will be impossible for us to visit him, if you do not.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet lowers his book, a sly smile playing on his lips.

elements.line: You are over scrupulous, surely. I dare say Mr. Bingley will be very glad to see you; and I will send a few lines by you to assure him of my hearty consent to his marrying whichever he chooses of the girls--though I must throw in a good word for my little Lizzy.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet's face flushes with irritation.

elements.line: I desire you will do no such thing. Lizzy is not a bit better than the others.

elements.text: CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mrs. Bennet criticizes Lizzy; Mr. Bennet defends her quickness; Mrs. Bennet complains about her nerves.

elements.line: I am sure she is not half so handsome as Jane, nor half so good-humoured as Lydia. But you are always giving her the preference.
They have none of them much to recommend them: they are all silly and ignorant like other girls; but Lizzy has something more of quickness than her sisters.
Mr. Bennet, how can you abuse your own children in such a way? You take delight in vexing me. You have no compassion on my poor nerves.
You mistake me, my dear. I have a high respect for your nerves. They are my old friends. I have heard you mention them with consideration these twenty years at least.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet sighs dramatically, pressing a hand to her temple.

elements.line: Ah, you do not know what I suffer.

elements.text: CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mr. Bennet sarcastically hopes she'll see many wealthy young men; narrator describes their characters.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet, seated in his armchair, looks up from his book with a dry smile.

elements.line: But I hope you will get over it, and live to see many young men of four thousand a year come into the neighbourhood.
It will be no use to us, if twenty such should come, since you will not visit them.
Depend upon it, my dear, that when there are twenty, I will visit them all.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet shakes her head, exasperated. Mr. Bennet returns to his book, a faint smirk on his lips.
A brief pause. The narrator's voice (V.O.) reflects on Mr. Bennet's character.

V.O.: Mr. Bennet was so odd a mixture of quick parts, sarcastic humour, reserve, and caprice, that the experience of three-and-twenty years had been insufficient to make his wife understand his character.
Mrs. Bennet sighs and turns away, muttering under her breath.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mr. Bennet reveals he visited Mr. Bingley, surprising his wife and daughters.

elements.text: The Bennet family sits in the sitting room. Lizzy is trimming a hat. Mr. Bennet watches her from his chair, a sly smile playing on his lips.

elements.line: I hope Mr. Bingley will like it, Lizzy.

elements.text: Lizzy looks up, puzzled. Mrs. Bennet stops her pacing and stares at her husband.

elements.line: We are not in a way to know what Mr. Bingley likes, since we are not to visit.
But you forget, mamma, that we shall meet him at the assemblies, and that Mrs. Long promised to introduce him.
I do not believe Mrs. Long will do any such thing. She has two nieces of her own. She is a selfish, hypocritical woman, and I have no opinion of her.
No, no, do not imagine it. I know Mrs. Long well, and she is a very good woman. She has two nieces of her own, but she would not do such a thing.
She has two nieces of her own. She is a selfish, hypocritical woman, and I have no opinion of her.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet begins to protest, but Mr. Bennet cuts her off with a wave of his hand.

elements.line: I cannot see that London has any great advantage over the country, for my part, except the shops and public places. The country is a vast deal pleasanter, is it not, Mr. Bingley?

elements.text: He pauses, letting the name hang in the air. Mrs. Bennet's eyes widen.

elements.line: Mr. Bingley? But you said you would not go!
I did. But I changed my mind. I waited on him this morning.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet is speechless. Lizzy smiles knowingly.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mrs. Bennet complains about not visiting Mr. Bingley, and the family discusses the upcoming ball.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet sits stiffly, her face resentful.

elements.line: We are not in a way to know what Mr. Bingley likes, since we are not to visit.
But you forget, mamma, that we shall meet him at the assemblies, and that Mrs. Long has promised to introduce him.
I do not believe Mrs. Long will do any such thing. She has two nieces of her own. She is a selfish, hypocritical woman, and I have no opinion of her.
No more have I; and I am glad to find that you do not depend on her serving you.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet purses her lips, then turns sharply to Kitty, who is coughing.

elements.line: Don't keep coughing so, Kitty, for heaven's sake! Have a little compassion on my nerves. You tear them to pieces.
Kitty has no discretion in her coughs; she times them ill.
I do not cough for my own amusement. When is your next ball to be, Lizzy?

elements.text: CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mrs. Bennet worries about introductions, and Mr. Bennet teases her.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet's eyes widen as she calculates. She shakes her head.

elements.line: To-morrow fortnight.
Ay, so it is, and Mrs. Long does not come back till the day before; so, it will be impossible for her to introduce him, for she will not know him herself.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet lowers his book, a sly smile playing on his lips.

elements.line: Then, my dear, you may have the advantage of your friend, and introduce Mr. Bingley to her.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet throws up her hands in exasperation.

elements.line: Impossible, Mr. Bennet, impossible, when I am not acquainted with him myself; how can you be so teasing?

elements.text: Mr. Bennet returns to his book, a dry chuckle escaping him.

elements.line: I honour your circumspection. A fortnight's acquaintance is certainly very little.

elements.text: CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mr. Bennet teases Mrs. Bennet and asks Mary for her opinion.

elements.text: The girls stare at their father. Mrs. Bennet says only, "Nonsense, nonsense!"

elements.line: What can be the meaning of that emphatic exclamation? Do you consider the forms of introduction, and the stress that is laid on them, as nonsense? I cannot quite agree with you there. What say you, Mary? For you are a young lady of deep reflection, I know, and read great books, and make extracts.

elements.text: Mary looks thoughtful, then speaks.

scenes.synopsis: Mary无法回答，Mrs. Bennet宣称她厌烦了Mr. Bingley。

elements.text: Mary opens her mouth, then closes it. She looks down at her book, fingers tracing the page edge.

elements.line: While Mary is adjusting her ideas, let us return to Mr. Bingley.
I am sick of Mr. Bingley!

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet throws her hands up and turns away from her husband.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mr. Bennet reveals he already visited Mr. Bingley, causing Mrs. Bennet's joy.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet closes his book and looks at his wife with a sly smile.

elements.line: I am sorry to hear that; but why did you not tell me so before? If I had known as much this morning, I certainly would not have called on him. It is very unlucky; but as I have actually paid the visit, we cannot escape the acquaintance now.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet's eyes widen, then she breaks into a delighted smile.

elements.line: How good it was in you, my dear Mr. Bennet! But I knew I should persuade you at last.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet chuckles and returns to his book, a knowing look on his face.
FADE OUT

scenes.synopsis: Mr. Bennet leaves the room; Mrs. Bennet praises him and boasts about Mr. Bingley dancing with Lydia.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet, weary of his wife's raptures, speaks to Kitty before exiting the room.

elements.line: Now, Kitty, you may cough as much as you choose.

elements.text: He leaves the room, closing the door behind him.

elements.line: What an excellent father you have, girls. I do not know how you will ever make him amends for his kindness; or me either, for that matter. At our time of life, it is not so pleasant, I can tell you, to be making new acquaintances every day; but for your sakes we would do anything. Lydia, my love, though you are the youngest, I dare say Mr. Bingley will dance with you at the next ball.
Oh, I am not afraid; for though I am the youngest, I'm the tallest.

elements.text: FADE OUT

scenes.synopsis: The Bennet family spends the evening speculating about Mr. Bingley's return visit and planning a dinner invitation.

elements.text: The Bennet family gathers in the sitting room after dinner. Mrs. Bennet paces excitedly while Mr. Bennet reads by the fire. The younger girls sit on the sofa, whispering.

elements.line: I am sure he will call tomorrow. Lady Lucas said he was so agreeable at the assembly!
Perhaps he may not come so soon. He has many acquaintances in the neighbourhood.
Nonsense, Jane! He danced with you twice. He must be eager to see you again.

elements.text: Lydia and Kitty giggle. Mary looks up from her book, then returns to reading. Mr. Bennet turns a page without looking up.

elements.line: We must decide when to ask him to dinner. Perhaps next week?
Yes, yes! I shall write a note tomorrow. We must have him before the Lucases do.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet continues to plan aloud while the others exchange glances. Mr. Bennet remains absorbed in his book, a faint smile on his lips.
FADE OUT

scenes.synopsis: Mrs. Bennet fails to get information about Mr. Bingley from her husband, then learns from Lady Lucas that Mr. Bingley is young, handsome, and will attend the next assembly.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet and her daughters surround Mr. Bennet in the library, firing questions about Mr. Bingley. Mr. Bennet remains absorbed in his book, responding with noncommittal grunts.
They try direct questions, clever suppositions, and vague hints, but Mr. Bennet deflects each with a dry remark or a turn of the page.
Defeated, Mrs. Bennet turns to Lady Lucas, who has just arrived. Lady Lucas reports that Sir William was delighted with Mr. Bingley.

elements.line: He is quite young, wonderfully handsome, extremely agreeable, and, to crown the whole, he means to be at the next assembly with a large party.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet's eyes light up. She clasps her hands together.

elements.line: To be fond of dancing is a certain step towards falling in love.

elements.text: She turns to Mr. Bennet with a hopeful smile.

elements.line: If I can but see one of my daughters happily settled at Netherfield, and all the others equally well married, I shall have nothing to wish for.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet looks up from his book, a faint smile on his lips, then returns to reading.
FADE OUT

scenes.synopsis: Mr. Bingley visits Mr. Bennet but only sees the father; the ladies observe him from an upper window.

elements.text: Mr. Bingley enters Mr. Bennet's library. Mr. Bennet rises from his chair, book in hand.
Mr. Bingley glances around the room, hoping to see the young ladies.
He sees only Mr. Bennet, who gestures to a chair. They sit.
Upstairs, Mrs. Bennet and her daughters crowd at an upper window, peering down.
They watch as Mr. Bingley mounts his black horse and rides away, his blue coat visible.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mrs. Bennet sends a dinner invitation to Mr. Bingley, but he declines due to business in town; Lady Lucas suggests he went to London to get a party for the ball.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet sits at her desk, penning a letter with eager strokes. She pauses, counting courses on her fingers.
A servant enters with a letter. Mrs. Bennet snatches it, reads, and her face falls. She slumps into a chair.

elements.line: What business can he possibly have in town so soon after arriving? He'll be flying about from place to place, never settled at Netherfield!

elements.text: Lady Lucas, seated nearby, sets down her teacup with a knowing smile.

elements.line: Perhaps he has gone to London only to get a large party for the ball.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet's expression brightens. She nods slowly, a new hope dawning.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: A report says Mr. Bingley will bring twelve ladies and seven gentlemen, but the day before the ball they learn he brings only six: his five sisters and a cousin.

elements.text: The Bennet sisters gather in the sitting room, their faces falling as they hear the news.
A few days later, the day before the ball, they receive updated information. Their expressions lighten.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Bingley's party enters the assembly-room; Darcy is admired for his looks and wealth but soon disliked for his proud manners.

elements.text: The assembly-room doors open. Mr. Bingley enters with his party: his two sisters, Mr. Hurst, and Mr. Darcy.
Bingley smiles warmly, nodding to acquaintances. His sisters glide in with fashionable airs. Mr. Hurst follows, looking gentlemanly but unremarkable.
Darcy enters last, tall and handsome. Heads turn. Whispers spread.
A cluster of gentlemen nod approvingly at Darcy. Nearby ladies whisper, glancing between him and Bingley.
For half the evening, Darcy is admired. Then he stands apart, refusing to dance, looking disdainfully around. His expression turns cold.
Guests exchange disapproving glances. A lady shakes her head. The admiration fades.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mr. Bingley dances with everyone and is well-liked; Mr. Darcy dances only with his party and is deemed proud and disagreeable.

elements.text: Mr. Bingley moves through the crowd, shaking hands and smiling. He joins a dance, laughing with his partner.
The ball ends. Bingley looks disappointed, glancing at the clock.
Bingley gestures animatedly to his friends, as if planning a future event.
Nearby, Mr. Darcy stands apart, arms crossed. He dances once with Mrs. Hurst, then once with Miss Bingley, then retreats to the edge of the room.
Guests whisper and shake their heads, casting disapproving glances at Darcy.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Mrs. Bennet resents Darcy for slighting Elizabeth; Elizabeth overhears Darcy telling Bingley he detests dancing at the assembly.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet glares at Darcy from across the room, her face flushed with indignation. She mutters to herself, shaking her head.
Elizabeth sits down after a dance, fanning herself. Nearby, Mr. Darcy stands alone, watching the crowd with a bored expression. Mr. Bingley approaches him.

elements.line: Come, Darcy, I must have you dance. I hate to see you standing about by yourself in this stupid manner. You had much better dance.

I certainly shall not. You know how I detest it, unless I am particularly acquainted with my partner. At such an assembly as this, it would be insupportable. Your sisters are engaged, and there is not another woman in the room whom it would not be a punishment to me to stand up with.

elements.text: Elizabeth's eyes widen slightly. She turns her head away, a faint blush rising on her cheeks. She clenches her fan.
Mrs. Bennet, having overheard, purses her lips and shoots a venomous look at Darcy.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Bingley urges Darcy to dance, but Darcy refuses and says Elizabeth is tolerable but not handsome enough to tempt him.

elements.text: Bingley and Darcy stand near a wall, observing the dancers. Bingley gestures enthusiastically toward the room.

elements.line: I would not be so fastidious as you are, for a kingdom! Upon my honour, I never met with so many pleasant girls in my life as I have this evening; and there are several of them, you see, uncommonly pretty.

elements.text: Darcy's gaze drifts to Jane Bennet, who is dancing nearby.

elements.line: You are dancing with the only handsome girl in the room.
elements.parenthetical: looking at the eldest Miss Bennet

elements.line: Oh, she is the most beautiful creature I ever beheld! But there is one of her sisters sitting down just behind you, who is very pretty, and I dare say very agreeable. Do let me ask my partner to introduce you.

elements.text: Darcy turns around and briefly looks at Elizabeth, who is seated nearby. Their eyes meet for a moment; Elizabeth's expression is curious. Darcy quickly looks away, his face cold.

elements.line: She is tolerable: but not handsome enough to tempt me; and I am in no humour at present to give consequence to young ladies who are slighted by other men. You had better return to your partner and enjoy her smiles, for you are wasting your time with me.

elements.text: Elizabeth, having overheard, stiffens slightly, her eyes narrowing with a mix of surprise and disdain. She turns away, pretending not to have heard.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: Elizabeth tells her friends the story of Darcy's slight with spirit.

elements.text: Mr. Bingley follows his advice, walking away. Mr. Darcy strides off in the opposite direction. Elizabeth remains standing alone, her gaze following Darcy with a hint of displeasure.
She turns to a group of friends nearby, a mischievous glint in her eyes. She begins to recount the incident, her voice animated and her gestures lively, mimicking Darcy's haughty tone.

elements.line: He said I was tolerable, but not handsome enough to tempt him!

elements.text: Her friends gasp and giggle. Elizabeth continues, her eyes sparkling with mock indignation.

elements.line: And he refused to dance with me! Can you imagine such pride?

elements.text: The group erupts in laughter. Elizabeth glances over her shoulder at Darcy, who is now standing alone across the room, unaware of the amusement he has caused.
CUT TO

scenes.synopsis: The Bennet family returns home in good spirits after a pleasant ball.

elements.text: The Bennet family walks along the dark lane toward Longbourn. Mrs. Bennet chatters excitedly, her voice carrying in the night air. Jane walks arm-in-arm with Elizabeth, a quiet smile on her face.

elements.line: Did you see how Mr. Bingley danced with Jane twice? And his sisters were so attentive to her!

elements.text: Jane blushes and looks down, but her smile widens. Elizabeth squeezes her arm affectionately.

elements.line: Indeed, Jane, you were the belle of the ball.

elements.text: Mrs. Bennet continues, barely pausing for breath. She gestures animatedly as she walks.

elements.line: And Mary was mentioned as the most accomplished girl in the neighbourhood! And Lydia and Kitty never lacked partners—excellent!

elements.text: The group approaches the front door of Longbourn. The warm glow of candlelight spills from the windows. Mrs. Bennet bustles inside, still talking, as Jane and Elizabeth follow more slowly, sharing a knowing look.
FADE OUT

scenes.synopsis: Mrs. Bennet recounts the ball to Mr. Bennet, praising Mr. Bingley and criticizing Mr. Darcy.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet sits in his armchair, a book open on his lap. He looks up as Mrs. Bennet bursts into the room, followed by Jane and Elizabeth.

elements.line: Oh, my dear Mr. Bennet, we have had a most delightful evening, a most excellent ball. I wish you had been there. Jane was so admired, nothing could be like it. Everybody said how well she looked; and Mr. Bingley thought her quite beautiful, and danced with her twice. Only think of that, my dear: he actually danced with her twice; and she was the only creature in the room that he asked a second time. First of all, he asked Miss Lucas. I was so vexed to see him stand up with her; but, however, he did not admire her at all; indeed, nobody can, you know; and he seemed quite struck with Jane as she was going down the dance. So he inquired who she was, and got introduced, and asked her for the two next. Then, the two third he danced with Miss King, and the two fourth with Maria Lucas, and the two fifth with Jane again, and the two sixth with Lizzy, and the Boulanger----
If he had had any compassion for me, he would not have danced half so much! For God's sake, say no more of his partners. O that he had sprained his ancle in the first dance!

Oh, my dear, I am quite delighted with him. He is so excessively handsome! and his sisters are charming women. I never in my life saw anything more elegant than their dresses. I dare say the lace upon Mrs. Hurst's gown----

elements.text: Mr. Bennet holds up a hand, cutting her off with a dismissive gesture.

elements.line: But I can assure you that Lizzy does not lose much by not suiting his fancy; for he is a most disagreeable, horrid man, not at all worth pleasing. So high and so conceited, that there was no enduring him! He walked here, and he walked there, fancying himself so very great! Not handsome enough to dance with! I wish you had been there, my dear, to have given him one of your set-downs. I quite detest the man.

elements.text: Mr. Bennet returns to his book, a faint smile playing on his lips.
FADE OUT

chapters.title: Chapter 1.
chapters.text:
It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession
of a good fortune must be in want of a wife.

However little known the feelings or views of such a man may be on his
first entering a neighbourhood, this truth is so well fixed in the minds
of the surrounding families, that he is considered as the rightful
property of some one or other of their daughters.

“My dear Mr. Bennet,” said his lady to him one day, “have you heard that
Netherfield Park is let at last?”

Mr. Bennet replied that he had not.

“But it is,” returned she; “for Mrs. Long has just been here, and she
told me all about it.”

Mr. Bennet made no answer.

“Do not you want to know who has taken it?” cried his wife, impatiently.

“_You_ want to tell me, and I have no objection to hearing it.”

[Illustration:

“He came down to see the place”

[_Copyright 1894 by George Allen._]]

This was invitation enough.

“Why, my dear, you must know, Mrs. Long says that Netherfield is taken
by a young man of large fortune from the north of England; that he came
down on Monday in a chaise and four to see the place, and was so much
delighted with it that he agreed with Mr. Morris immediately; that he is
to take possession before Michaelmas, and some of his servants are to be
in the house by the end of next week.”

“What is his name?”

“Bingley.”

“Is he married or single?”

“Oh, single, my dear, to be sure! A single man of large fortune; four or
five thousand a year. What a fine thing for our girls!”

“How so? how can it affect them?”

“My dear Mr. Bennet,” replied his wife, “how can you be so tiresome? You
must know that I am thinking of his marrying one of them.”

“Is that his design in settling here?”

“Design? Nonsense, how can you talk so! But it is very likely that he
_may_ fall in love with one of them, and therefore you must visit him as
soon as he comes.”

“I see no occasion for that. You and the girls may go--or you may send
them by themselves, which perhaps will be still better; for as you are
as handsome as any of them, Mr. Bingley might like you the best of the
party.”

“My dear, you flatter me. I certainly _have_ had my share of beauty, but
I do not pretend to be anything extraordinary now. When a woman has five
grown-up daughters, she ought to give over thinking of her own beauty.”

“In such cases, a woman has not often much beauty to think of.”

“But, my dear, you must indeed go and see Mr. Bingley when he comes into
the neighbourhood.”

“It is more than I engage for, I assure you.”

“But consider your daughters. Only think what an establishment it would
be for one of them. Sir William and Lady Lucas are determined to go,
merely on that account; for in general, you know, they visit no new
comers. Indeed you must go, for it will be impossible for _us_ to visit
him, if you do not.”

“You are over scrupulous, surely. I dare say Mr. Bingley will be very
glad to see you; and I will send a few lines by you to assure him of my
hearty consent to his marrying whichever he chooses of the girls--though
I must throw in a good word for my little Lizzy.”

“I desire you will do no such thing. Lizzy is not a bit better than the
others: and I am sure she is not half so handsome as Jane, nor half so
good-humoured as Lydia. But you are always giving _her_ the preference.”

“They have none of them much to recommend them,” replied he: “they are
all silly and ignorant like other girls; but Lizzy has something more of
quickness than her sisters.”

“Mr. Bennet, how can you abuse your own children in such a way? You take
delight in vexing me. You have no compassion on my poor nerves.”

“You mistake me, my dear. I have a high respect for your nerves. They
are my old friends. I have heard you mention them with consideration
these twenty years at least.”

“Ah, you do not know what I suffer.”

“But I hope you will get over it, and live to see many young men of four
thousand a year come into the neighbourhood.”

“It will be no use to us, if twenty such should come, since you will not
visit them.”

“Depend upon it, my dear, that when there are twenty, I will visit them
all.”

Mr. Bennet was so odd a mixture of quick parts, sarcastic humour,
reserve, and caprice, that the experience of three-and-twenty years had
been insufficient to make his wife understand his character. _Her_ mind
was less difficult to develope. She was a woman of mean understanding,
little information, and uncertain temper. When she was discontented, she
fancied herself nervous. The business of her life was to get her
daughters married: its solace was visiting and news.

[Illustration: M^{r.} & M^{rs.} Bennet

[_Copyright 1894 by George Allen._]]




[Illustration:

“I hope Mr. Bingley will like it”

[_Copyright 1894 by George Allen._]]

chapters.title: CHAPTER II.
chapters.text:
[Illustration]

Mr. Bennet was among the earliest of those who waited on Mr. Bingley. He
had always intended to visit him, though to the last always assuring his
wife that he should not go; and till the evening after the visit was
paid she had no knowledge of it. It was then disclosed in the following
manner. Observing his second daughter employed in trimming a hat, he
suddenly addressed her with,--

“I hope Mr. Bingley will like it, Lizzy.”

“We are not in a way to know _what_ Mr. Bingley likes,” said her mother,
resentfully, “since we are not to visit.”

“But you forget, mamma,” said Elizabeth, “that we shall meet him at the
assemblies, and that Mrs. Long has promised to introduce him.”

“I do not believe Mrs. Long will do any such thing. She has two nieces
of her own. She is a selfish, hypocritical woman, and I have no opinion
of her.”

“No more have I,” said Mr. Bennet; “and I am glad to find that you do
not depend on her serving you.”

Mrs. Bennet deigned not to make any reply; but, unable to contain
herself, began scolding one of her daughters.

“Don’t keep coughing so, Kitty, for heaven’s sake! Have a little
compassion on my nerves. You tear them to pieces.”

“Kitty has no discretion in her coughs,” said her father; “she times
them ill.”

“I do not cough for my own amusement,” replied Kitty, fretfully. “When
is your next ball to be, Lizzy?”

“To-morrow fortnight.”

“Ay, so it is,” cried her mother, “and Mrs. Long does not come back till
the day before; so, it will be impossible for her to introduce him, for
she will not know him herself.”

“Then, my dear, you may have the advantage of your friend, and introduce
Mr. Bingley to _her_.”

“Impossible, Mr. Bennet, impossible, when I am not acquainted with him
myself; how can you be so teasing?”

“I honour your circumspection. A fortnight’s acquaintance is certainly
very little. One cannot know what a man really is by the end of a
fortnight. But if _we_ do not venture, somebody else will; and after
all, Mrs. Long and her nieces must stand their chance; and, therefore,
as she will think it an act of kindness, if you decline the office, I
will take it on myself.”

The girls stared at their father. Mrs. Bennet said only, “Nonsense,
nonsense!”

“What can be the meaning of that emphatic exclamation?” cried he. “Do
you consider the forms of introduction, and the stress that is laid on
them, as nonsense? I cannot quite agree with you _there_. What say you,
Mary? For you are a young lady of deep reflection, I know, and read
great books, and make extracts.”

Mary wished to say something very sensible, but knew not how.

“While Mary is adjusting her ideas,” he continued, “let us return to Mr.
Bingley.”

“I am sick of Mr. Bingley,” cried his wife.

“I am sorry to hear _that_; but why did you not tell me so before? If I
had known as much this morning, I certainly would not have called on
him. It is very unlucky; but as I have actually paid the visit, we
cannot escape the acquaintance now.”

The astonishment of the ladies was just what he wished--that of Mrs.
Bennet perhaps surpassing the rest; though when the first tumult of joy
was over, she began to declare that it was what she had expected all the
while.

“How good it was in you, my dear Mr. Bennet! But I knew I should
persuade you at last. I was sure you loved your girls too well to
neglect such an acquaintance. Well, how pleased I am! And it is such a
good joke, too, that you should have gone this morning, and never said a
word about it till now.”

“Now, Kitty, you may cough as much as you choose,” said Mr. Bennet; and,
as he spoke, he left the room, fatigued with the raptures of his wife.

“What an excellent father you have, girls,” said she, when the door was
shut. “I do not know how you will ever make him amends for his kindness;
or me either, for that matter. At our time of life, it is not so
pleasant, I can tell you, to be making new acquaintances every day; but
for your sakes we would do anything. Lydia, my love, though you _are_
the youngest, I dare say Mr. Bingley will dance with you at the next
ball.”

“Oh,” said Lydia, stoutly, “I am not afraid; for though I _am_ the
youngest, I’m the tallest.”

The rest of the evening was spent in conjecturing how soon he would
return Mr. Bennet’s visit, and determining when they should ask him to
dinner.

[Illustration: “I’m the tallest”]




[Illustration:

     “He rode a black horse”
]

chapters.title: CHAPTER III.
chapters.text:
[Illustration]

Not all that Mrs. Bennet, however, with the assistance of her five
daughters, could ask on the subject, was sufficient to draw from her
husband any satisfactory description of Mr. Bingley. They attacked him
in various ways, with barefaced questions, ingenious suppositions, and
distant surmises; but he eluded the skill of them all; and they were at
last obliged to accept the second-hand intelligence of their neighbour,
Lady Lucas. Her report was highly favourable. Sir William had been
delighted with him. He was quite young, wonderfully handsome, extremely
agreeable, and, to crown the whole, he meant to be at the next assembly
with a large party. Nothing could be more delightful! To be fond of
dancing was a certain step towards falling in love; and very lively
hopes of Mr. Bingley’s heart were entertained.

“If I can but see one of my daughters happily settled at Netherfield,”
said Mrs. Bennet to her husband, “and all the others equally well
married, I shall have nothing to wish for.”

In a few days Mr. Bingley returned Mr. Bennet’s visit, and sat about ten
minutes with him in his library. He had entertained hopes of being
admitted to a sight of the young ladies, of whose beauty he had heard
much; but he saw only the father. The ladies were somewhat more
fortunate, for they had the advantage of ascertaining, from an upper
window, that he wore a blue coat and rode a black horse.

An invitation to dinner was soon afterwards despatched; and already had
Mrs. Bennet planned the courses that were to do credit to her
housekeeping, when an answer arrived which deferred it all. Mr. Bingley
was obliged to be in town the following day, and consequently unable to
accept the honour of their invitation, etc. Mrs. Bennet was quite
disconcerted. She could not imagine what business he could have in town
so soon after his arrival in Hertfordshire; and she began to fear that
he might always be flying about from one place to another, and never
settled at Netherfield as he ought to be. Lady Lucas quieted her fears a
little by starting the idea of his

[Illustration:

     “When the Party entered”

[_Copyright 1894 by George Allen._]]

being gone to London only to get a large party for the ball; and a
report soon followed that Mr. Bingley was to bring twelve ladies and
seven gentlemen with him to the assembly. The girls grieved over such a
number of ladies; but were comforted the day before the ball by hearing
that, instead of twelve, he had brought only six with him from London,
his five sisters and a cousin. And when the party entered the
assembly-room, it consisted of only five altogether: Mr. Bingley, his
two sisters, the husband of the eldest, and another young man.

Mr. Bingley was good-looking and gentlemanlike: he had a pleasant
countenance, and easy, unaffected manners. His sisters were fine women,
with an air of decided fashion. His brother-in-law, Mr. Hurst, merely
looked the gentleman; but his friend Mr. Darcy soon drew the attention
of the room by his fine, tall person, handsome features, noble mien, and
the report, which was in general circulation within five minutes after
his entrance, of his having ten thousand a year. The gentlemen
pronounced him to be a fine figure of a man, the ladies declared he was
much handsomer than Mr. Bingley, and he was looked at with great
admiration for about half the evening, till his manners gave a disgust
which turned the tide of his popularity; for he was discovered to be
proud, to be above his company, and above being pleased; and not all his
large estate in Derbyshire could save him from having a most forbidding,
disagreeable countenance, and being unworthy to be compared with his
friend.

Mr. Bingley had soon made himself acquainted with all the principal
people in the room: he was lively and unreserved, danced every dance,
was angry that the ball closed so early, and talked of giving one
himself at Netherfield. Such amiable qualities must speak for
themselves. What a contrast between him and his friend! Mr. Darcy danced
only once with Mrs. Hurst and once with Miss Bingley, declined being
introduced to any other lady, and spent the rest of the evening in
walking about the room, speaking occasionally to one of his own party.
His character was decided. He was the proudest, most disagreeable man in
the world, and everybody hoped that he would never come there again.
Amongst the most violent against him was Mrs. Bennet, whose dislike of
his general behaviour was sharpened into particular resentment by his
having slighted one of her daughters.

Elizabeth Bennet had been obliged, by the scarcity of gentlemen, to sit
down for two dances; and during part of that time, Mr. Darcy had been
standing near enough for her to overhear a conversation between him and
Mr. Bingley, who came from the dance for a few minutes to press his
friend to join it.

“Come, Darcy,” said he, “I must have you dance. I hate to see you
standing about by yourself in this stupid manner. You had much better
dance.”

“I certainly shall not. You know how I detest it, unless I am
particularly acquainted with my partner. At such an assembly as this, it
would be insupportable. Your sisters are engaged, and there is not
another woman in the room whom it would not be a punishment to me to
stand up with.”

“I would not be so fastidious as you are,” cried Bingley, “for a
kingdom! Upon my honour, I never met with so many pleasant girls in my
life as I have this evening; and there are several of them, you see,
uncommonly pretty.”

“_You_ are dancing with the only handsome girl in the room,” said Mr.
Darcy, looking at the eldest Miss Bennet.

“Oh, she is the most beautiful creature I ever beheld! But there is one
of her sisters sitting down just behind you, who is very pretty, and I
dare say very agreeable. Do let me ask my partner to introduce you.”

[Illustration:

“She is tolerable”

[_Copyright 1894 by George Allen._]]

“Which do you mean?” and turning round, he looked for a moment at
Elizabeth, till, catching her eye, he withdrew his own, and coldly said,
“She is tolerable: but not handsome enough to tempt _me_; and I am in no
humour at present to give consequence to young ladies who are slighted
by other men. You had better return to your partner and enjoy her
smiles, for you are wasting your time with me.”

Mr. Bingley followed his advice. Mr. Darcy walked off; and Elizabeth
remained with no very cordial feelings towards him. She told the story,
however, with great spirit among her friends; for she had a lively,
playful disposition, which delighted in anything ridiculous.

The evening altogether passed off pleasantly to the whole family. Mrs.
Bennet had seen her eldest daughter much admired by the Netherfield
party. Mr. Bingley had danced with her twice, and she had been
distinguished by his sisters. Jane was as much gratified by this as her
mother could be, though in a quieter way. Elizabeth felt Jane’s
pleasure. Mary had heard herself mentioned to Miss Bingley as the most
accomplished girl in the neighbourhood; and Catherine and Lydia had been
fortunate enough to be never without partners, which was all that they
had yet learnt to care for at a ball. They returned, therefore, in good
spirits to Longbourn, the village where they lived, and of which they
were the principal inhabitants. They found Mr. Bennet still up. With a
book, he was regardless of time; and on the present occasion he had a
good deal of curiosity as to the event of an evening which had raised
such splendid expectations. He had rather hoped that all his wife’s
views on the stranger would be disappointed; but he soon found that he
had a very different story to hear.

“Oh, my dear Mr. Bennet,” as she entered the room, “we have had a most
delightful evening, a most excellent ball. I wish you had been there.
Jane was so admired, nothing could be like it. Everybody said how well
she looked; and Mr. Bingley thought her quite beautiful, and danced with
her twice. Only think of _that_, my dear: he actually danced with her
twice; and she was the only creature in the room that he asked a second
time. First of all, he asked Miss Lucas. I was so vexed to see him stand
up with her; but, however, he did not admire her at all; indeed, nobody
can, you know; and he seemed quite struck with Jane as she was going
down the dance. So he inquired who she was, and got introduced, and
asked her for the two next. Then, the two third he danced with Miss
King, and the two fourth with Maria Lucas, and the two fifth with Jane
again, and the two sixth with Lizzy, and the _Boulanger_----”

“If he had had any compassion for _me_,” cried her husband impatiently,
“he would not have danced half so much! For God’s sake, say no more of
his partners. O that he had sprained his ancle in the first dance!”

“Oh, my dear,” continued Mrs. Bennet, “I am quite delighted with him. He
is so excessively handsome! and his sisters are charming women. I never
in my life saw anything more elegant than their dresses. I dare say the
lace upon Mrs. Hurst’s gown----”

Here she was interrupted again. Mr. Bennet protested against any
description of finery. She was therefore obliged to seek another branch
of the subject, and related, with much bitterness of spirit, and some
exaggeration, the shocking rudeness of Mr. Darcy.

“But I can assure you,” she added, “that Lizzy does not lose much by not
suiting _his_ fancy; for he is a most disagreeable, horrid man, not at
all worth pleasing. So high and so conceited, that there was no enduring
him! He walked here, and he walked there, fancying himself so very
great! Not handsome enough to dance with! I wish you had been there, my
dear, to have given him one of your set-downs. I quite detest the man.”




[Illustration]
